sourceMovie.title: Sicario: Day of the Soldado
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Sicario: Day of the Soldado works as a action movie with a point of view—fans quote moments, but they also argue about what the film is actually saying about power, desire, or survival. The action and crime blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Sicario
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Sicario: Day of the Soldado's action stayed with you, Sicario (2015) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The crime through-line is the real common thread between them.

recommendations.title: The Transporter
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Where Sicario: Day of the Soldado excels in action, The Transporter (2002) doubles down on crime to comparable effect. The tonal similarities run deep enough that watching both back-to-back feels intentional.

recommendations.title: Desperado
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Desperado (1995) is the natural next step after Sicario: Day of the Soldado: same action DNA, different perspective. The dark streak and crime elevate it well above a simple genre exercise.

recommendations.title: Once Upon a Time in Mexico
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of Sicario: Day of the Soldado who respond to its action will find Once Upon a Time in Mexico (2003) immediately familiar. Both films share a crime sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

recommendations.title: The Jackal
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason Sicario: Day of the Soldado fans keep landing on The Jackal (1997) — the action is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

recommendations.title: Shoot 'Em Up
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Shoot 'Em Up draws from the same action well as Sicario: Day of the Soldado, channeling crime in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 2007 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

recommendations.title: War
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: War (2007) captures the action appeal of Sicario: Day of the Soldado without simply imitating it. The crime streak running through both films is what puts War at the top of any "if you liked…" shortlist.

recommendations.title: Assassins
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The action appeal of Sicario: Day of the Soldado — that dark edge, the crime — translates cleanly into Assassins (1995). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: The Getaway
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind The Getaway (1972) clearly understood what makes action work — the same quality that keeps Sicario: Day of the Soldado rewatchable. Expect the same dark rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

recommendations.title: The Way of the Gun
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Way of the Gun (2000) is the kind of film Sicario: Day of the Soldado fans discover and immediately send to their group chat. The action overlap is real, but it's the dark emotional pull that seals the recommendation.

faq.question: What should I watch after Sicario: Day of the Soldado?
faq.answer: Try Sicario, The Transporter, Desperado—each shares DNA with Sicario: Day of the Soldado while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Sicario: Day of the Soldado?
faq.answer: Sicario: Day of the Soldado (2018) is best described as Action, Crime, Thriller. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Sicario: Day of the Soldado part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Sicario Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
